title: REIT Dividend Yields 2026: Highest Yielding REITs Ranked
description: Discover which real estate sector is surprisingly offering the highest payouts right now. Explore our list of 50+ publicly traded real estate investment trusts ranked by yield to find hidden income opportunities.

schemas.headline: REIT Dividend Yields 2026: Highest Yielding REITs Ranked
schemas.description: Discover which real estate sector is surprisingly offering the highest payouts right now. Explore our list of 50+ publicly traded real estate investment trusts ranked by yield to find hidden income opportunities.

mainEntity.name: What are the highest yielding REITs in 2026?

acceptedAnswer.text: The highest yielding REITs currently vary by sector, with certain hotel, office, and mortgage REITs offering yields upwards of 7-9%. However, higher yields often come with elevated risks regarding payout sustainability and potential capital depreciation.

The safety of REIT dividend yields in 2026 depends heavily on the company's payout ratio and cash flow generation (FFO or AFFO). A payout ratio consistently above 100% or significantly higher than peers may indicate an unsustainable dividend that could face cuts.

mainEntity.name: What is the best REIT for dividends?

acceptedAnswer.text: The best REIT for dividends depends on an investor's goals. For high immediate income, certain specialty or healthcare REITs might be appealing. For reliable, long-term growth, many look towards established retail or industrial REITs with a history of consistent dividend increases.

mainEntity.name: How does sector affect REIT dividend yields?

acceptedAnswer.text: Different real estate sectors carry different risk profiles and growth prospects, which directly affect yields. For instance, office REITs may offer higher yields due to perceived risks regarding occupancy, while data center or industrial REITs might have lower starting yields but stronger growth potential.

mainEntity.name: Why do some REITs have very high payout ratios?

acceptedAnswer.text: REITs are legally required to distribute at least 90% of their taxable income to shareholders, naturally leading to high payout ratios compared to standard corporations. However, an exceptionally high payout ratio (e.g., over 150%) often signals earnings distress or a temporarily elevated dividend relative to current cash flows.
